refined_fr.refined_fr: Enable measurement and reporting of material throughput in volumetric or mass units per unit time.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) the system's capability to measure/report throughput metrics, and (2) the achievement of a customer-defined target. The latter is a success criterion dependent on operational execution, not a system requirement. The refined FR isolates the system's functional obligation—providing visibility into throughput—which is solution-neutral and testable independent of whether a specific productivity target is met. The target itself belongs in acceptance criteria or KPI definitions, not in the FR.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Demonstrate that the NGHE solution captures and reports material throughput (m³/h or ton/h) during representative operations. Verify data accuracy against independent measurement. Confirm reporting is accessible to Site Manager and Equipment Operator in real time or near-real time.
sub_frs: Acquire volumetric or mass flow data from material handling subsystems during operation.
Calculate throughput rate (m³/h or ton/h) from acquired flow data.
Display or transmit throughput metrics to authorized stakeholders in real time or near-real time.
Log historical throughput data for post-operation analysis and reporting.

candidate_dps: DP1: Acquire instantaneous flow rate via continuous sensing of material stream properties (mass or volume per unit time) and integrate over evaluation period to compute cumulative throughput
DP2: Measure discrete material batches (count, mass, or volume per batch) at regular intervals and extrapolate to throughput rate over evaluation period
DP3: Monitor material level change in a bounded reservoir (input or output) over time and derive throughput from level-delta and cross-sectional geometry
DP4: Combine multiple independent measurement modalities (e.g., batch counting + continuous sensing) with reconciliation logic to report throughput with confidence bounds
DP5: Instrument material transport mechanism (conveyor speed, pump displacement, bucket fill cycles) and compute throughput from mechanism kinematics and material properties
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP1: Acquire instantaneous flow rate via continuous sensing of material stream properties (mass or volume per unit time) and integrate over evaluation period to compute cumulative throughput

potential_sources: Sensor selection (mass vs. volumetric) is decoupled from flow-rate acquisition logic; choice depends on material type and acceptance criteria, not on measurement principle
Integration method (numerical summation, time-weighted averaging) is independent of sensor modality
Reporting format (units, time windows, confidence intervals) is a separate concern from acquisition and can be configured without redesign
Calibration and drift compensation are localized to sensor module; core throughput logic remains stable
coupling_check.notes: DP1 exhibits low coupling because the functional requirement (measure throughput) maps cleanly to a single logical solution (continuous flow sensing + integration). Alternative DPs (DP2, DP3, DP5) introduce dependencies: batch counting couples to batch-size uniformity; level-change sensing couples to reservoir geometry and material compaction; mechanism kinematics couples to transport equipment design. DP4 (multi-modal) increases complexity and introduces reconciliation logic that must handle sensor disagreement—acceptable only if acceptance criteria demand redundancy or high confidence.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Material stream is accessible for continuous sensing without disrupting operations or safety. RISK: If worksite layout or safety plan prohibits sensor placement in material path, DP1 may require non-contact sensing (e.g., radar, optical) with reduced accuracy.

ASSUMPTION: Material properties (density, particle size distribution) are stable or known during evaluation period. RISK: If material composition varies significantly, volumetric sensing may require real-time density correction; mass sensing is more robust but may require higher-precision instrumentation.

ASSUMPTION: Evaluation period is long enough (hours to days) that integration noise and sensor drift are negligible relative to throughput_target. RISK: If evaluation period is short (minutes) or throughput is highly variable, instantaneous flow-rate noise may exceed acceptable reporting error; DP2 (batch counting) may be more robust.

ASSUMPTION: Sensor calibration and maintenance can be performed within operational constraints. RISK: If worksite does not permit downtime for calibration, sensor drift may accumulate; DP3 (level-change) or DP5 (mechanism kinematics) may require less frequent recalibration.

ASSUMPTION: Acceptance criteria (reported productivity ≥ productivity_target) is based on absolute throughput, not relative accuracy. RISK: If criteria require ±5% accuracy, DP1 may require redundant sensing (DP4) or higher-grade instrumentation.

ASSUMPTION: Data logging and integration computation can be performed on-site or transmitted reliably. RISK: If connectivity is unreliable, local buffering and post-hoc integration (DP2 batch reconciliation) may be more practical.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR should be refined to specify: (1) Required measurement accuracy (e.g., ±10% of throughput_target) and confidence level (e.g., 95%); (2) Evaluation period duration and material variability profile; (3) Worksite constraints on sensor placement, power, and maintenance windows; (4) Whether throughput must be reported in real-time or post-evaluation. With these clarifications, DP1 remains the preferred solution for continuous, high-frequency material streams; DP2 or DP3 may be preferred for batch-oriented or low-frequency operations. Recommend pairing selected DP with explicit IM strategy: centralize flow-rate acquisition and integration logic in a single measurement module (hardware + firmware or software) to minimize coupling and enable independent validation against acceptance criteria.

im_boundary.name: Vehicle
im_boundary.scope: Onboard sensing, signal acquisition, and local integration of flow rate measurements to compute cumulative throughput; includes sensor interface, analog-to-digital conversion, and integration logic resident on the vehicle platform.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle transmits computed cumulative throughput and instantaneous flow rate samples to external systems (ground station, cloud backend, or teleoperation center) at periodic intervals.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle receives evaluation period duration, sampling rate, or flow measurement mode parameters from external tasking authority to configure local integration behavior.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle uses network link to transmit telemetry and receive tasking commands; provides transport for flow rate data egress and configuration ingress.

module_containment_scores.notes: Strong containment: sensing hardware, signal conditioning, and integration computation are naturally resident on the vehicle platform. Sensor acquisition and local processing are vehicle-owned. Minor diffusion: cumulative throughput results must be transmitted via Telemetry interface; tasking parameters must be received via Tasking interface. This is acceptable boundary diffusion (data flow, not logic duplication).

Weak containment: NGHE Solution is a high-level system wrapper. Placing sensor acquisition and integration logic here would require Vehicle to delegate sensing to NGHE, creating tight coupling and diffusing the core DP (continuous sensing and integration) across both modules. Not recommended.

Poor containment: Teleoperation is a command/control interface layer, not a sensing or computation module. Placing flow rate acquisition and integration here would require remote processing of sensor data, introducing latency, network dependency, and diffusion of real-time sensing logic away from the vehicle. Violates locality principle.

Poor containment: Safety Services is a cross-cutting concern for hazard monitoring and alerts. Flow rate measurement and integration is a functional measurement task, not a safety service. Placing it here would conflate measurement with safety logic and diffuse the DP across unrelated concerns.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Vehicle module is the natural owner of continuous flow rate sensing and cumulative throughput integration because: (1) sensor hardware and signal conditioning are physically resident on the vehicle; (2) local integration computation minimizes latency and network dependency; (3) the DP is cohesive within vehicle-level measurement and telemetry responsibilities; (4) external interfaces (Telemetry for results, Tasking for configuration, Network Link for transport) are well-established and appropriate for data egress and command ingress.
containment_risks: Risk: If sensor hardware is not yet specified or is externally mounted (e.g., on a fixed installation), the Vehicle boundary may not own the physical sensor. Mitigation: Clarify sensor ownership; if external, define a clear sensor interface (e.g., analog input, CAN bus) that Vehicle consumes.

Risk: If integration period or sampling rate must be dynamically adjusted by remote operators, Tasking interface dependency increases. Mitigation: Design Tasking protocol to support configuration updates; ensure Vehicle can apply new parameters without service interruption.

Risk: If flow rate data volume is high and network bandwidth is constrained, Telemetry transmission may become a bottleneck. Mitigation: Implement local buffering and compression; consider edge aggregation (e.g., periodic summaries) before transmission.

Risk: If Safety Services requires real-time flow rate alerts (e.g., overpressure, underflow), Vehicle must also expose flow rate data to Safety Services via an additional interface. Mitigation: Define a SafetyAlert interface for critical flow anomalies; keep integration logic in Vehicle, but allow Safety Services to subscribe to threshold events.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP1 directly addresses FR by specifying a concrete mechanism: continuous sensing of material stream properties (mass or volume per unit time) with integration over an evaluation period to compute cumulative throughput. The DP is solution-neutral (does not prescribe sensor type or integration algorithm) and is measurable against the FR's requirement for volumetric or mass units per unit time. Traceability is clear.

diffusion_risks: Minor diffusion: Cumulative throughput results egress via Telemetry interface and configuration ingress via Tasking interface. This is acceptable boundary diffusion (data flow, not logic duplication) and reflects proper separation of concerns between onboard measurement and external command/reporting.

Conditional risk: If Safety Services requires real-time flow rate alerts (e.g., overpressure, underflow detection), the flow rate data stream may need to be exposed to Safety Services via an additional interface. Current design does not show this coupling; if required, define a SafetyAlert subscription interface to avoid duplicating integration logic.

Conditional risk: If sensor hardware is externally mounted or not vehicle-resident, the Vehicle boundary assumption breaks. Mitigation: Clarify sensor ownership and define a clear sensor interface (analog input, CAN bus, etc.) that Vehicle consumes.

Conditional risk: High-volume flow rate data transmission via Telemetry may saturate network bandwidth. Mitigation: Implement local buffering, compression, or edge aggregation (periodic summaries) before transmission.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Vehicle module achieves 0.85 containment score with 0.15 diffusion. Strong containment justified: sensing hardware, signal conditioning, and integration computation are naturally resident on vehicle platform. Sensor acquisition and local processing are vehicle-owned. The DP (continuous sensing + integration) is cohesive within vehicle-level measurement responsibilities. External interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, Network Link) are appropriate for data egress and command ingress and do not represent logic diffusion. Alternative module candidates (NGHE Solution, Teleoperation, Safety Services) all exhibit poor containment (0.2–0.6) and high diffusion (0.6–0.8) due to architectural mismatch: they are either high-level wrappers, command/control layers, or cross-cutting concerns, not measurement/sensing modules. Vehicle is the correct owner.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification is feasible. Acceptance criterion 'reported productivity ≥ productivity_target over agreed evaluation period' maps directly to IM (Vehicle) output: cumulative throughput transmitted via Telemetry interface. Test protocol: (1) configure Vehicle via Tasking with known evaluation period and sampling rate; (2) operate material stream under controlled conditions; (3) collect Telemetry reports of cumulative throughput; (4) compare against ground-truth measurement or reference standard. Worksite safety plan and operational policies are external constraints that do not impede measurement verification.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear. FR is allocated to Vehicle IM via DP1. Vehicle owns sensor acquisition, signal conditioning, and integration computation. Tasking interface provides configuration authority (evaluation period, sampling rate, measurement mode). Telemetry interface provides results egress. Network Link provides transport. Ownership boundaries are well-defined. No ambiguity regarding which module is responsible for continuous sensing, integration, or result reporting.

recommended_revisions: Clarify sensor hardware ownership and mounting location. If sensor is external to vehicle, define a formal sensor interface (e.g., analog input voltage range, CAN message format, I2C protocol) that Vehicle consumes. Document sensor calibration and accuracy specifications.

Specify Tasking protocol for dynamic configuration updates (evaluation period, sampling rate, measurement mode). Define whether Vehicle can apply new parameters without service interruption or if a restart is required.

Define Telemetry message format and transmission frequency. Specify whether cumulative throughput is reported as a single summary per evaluation period or as periodic incremental updates. Address network bandwidth constraints via buffering or compression if necessary.

If Safety Services requires real-time flow rate alerts (overpressure, underflow, anomaly detection), define a SafetyAlert interface as a separate subscription channel. Keep integration logic in Vehicle; allow Safety Services to subscribe to threshold events without duplicating computation.

Document sensor accuracy, resolution, and latency requirements. Verify that selected sensor hardware and integration algorithm meet the acceptance criterion 'reported productivity ≥ productivity_target' with acceptable measurement uncertainty.
Add a verification plan that specifies test conditions, reference standards, and acceptance tolerances for cumulative throughput measurement during representative operations.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with strong technical justification and clear allocation to Vehicle IM. However, the high safety criticality tailoring factor and multiple conditional diffusion risks (Safety Services coupling, sensor hardware ownership, network bandwidth, measurement uncertainty) warrant decomposition of the current DP into lower-level sub-FRs to address these concerns explicitly. The recommended revisions cluster around four distinct functional concerns: (1) sensor acquisition and calibration, (2) dynamic configuration management, (3) result reporting and transport, and (4) safety-critical alerting. These are candidates for sub-level decomposition. Decomposition will expose safety-critical dependencies, clarify interface contracts, and enable independent verification of measurement accuracy—critical for high-safety-criticality systems. Proceeding to sub-level FRs will also resolve the conditional risks flagged in the DP-IM gate without requiring wholesale revision of the current level.
next_level_sub_frs: FR1.1: Acquire continuous material stream property measurements (mass or volume per unit time) from sensor hardware with specified accuracy, resolution, and latency.
FR1.2: Integrate acquired measurements over a configurable evaluation period to compute cumulative throughput.
FR1.3: Accept dynamic configuration updates (evaluation period, sampling rate, measurement mode) via Tasking interface without service interruption or with controlled restart.
FR1.4: Report cumulative throughput results via Telemetry interface with specified message format, transmission frequency, and network efficiency.
FR1.5: Detect and alert Safety Services to anomalous flow conditions (overpressure, underflow, measurement failure) in real-time via SafetyAlert subscription interface.

revise.why: Current DP1 ('continuous sensing of material stream properties with integration over evaluation period') is logically sound but insufficiently detailed to address safety-critical measurement accuracy, sensor hardware ownership, and safety alerting. Decomposition into sub-level DPs will specify: (a) sensor interface contract (analog voltage range, CAN format, I2C protocol, calibration procedure); (b) integration algorithm and numerical stability; (c) configuration protocol and state management; (d) Telemetry message schema and buffering strategy; (e) SafetyAlert threshold logic and latency bounds. These sub-DPs will be traceable to sub-FRs and will resolve conditional diffusion risks without invalidating the current-level DP.

assumptions: Sensor hardware is vehicle-resident or accessible via a well-defined interface (analog input, CAN bus, I2C) that Vehicle module owns or consumes.

Measurement accuracy and resolution of selected sensor hardware are sufficient to meet the acceptance criterion 'reported productivity ≥ productivity_target' with acceptable uncertainty (to be quantified in sub-level verification).

Safety Services module exists and is capable of subscribing to real-time flow rate alerts via a SafetyAlert interface; if Safety Services does not exist, safety alerting logic remains within Vehicle module.
Network bandwidth and Telemetry transport are adequate for the expected transmission frequency and message size; if not, local buffering or edge aggregation is acceptable.
Tasking interface supports dynamic parameter updates; if not, Vehicle may require service restart for configuration changes (to be clarified in sub-level DP).
Worksite safety plan and operational policies are external constraints that do not impede measurement verification or safety alerting.
open_risks: Safety-critical measurement accuracy: Sensor hardware selection and calibration procedure must be validated to ensure reported productivity meets acceptance criterion with acceptable measurement uncertainty. Sub-level verification plan required.

Safety alerting latency: If Safety Services requires real-time overpressure or underflow alerts, the latency of the SafetyAlert interface must be specified and verified. Current design does not quantify this; sub-level DP for FR1.5 must address.

Sensor hardware ownership and mounting: If sensor is external to vehicle or not vehicle-resident, a formal sensor interface contract (electrical, protocol, calibration) must be defined. Clarification required before sub-level DP design.

Network bandwidth saturation: High-volume flow rate data transmission via Telemetry may exceed available bandwidth. Sub-level DP for FR1.4 must specify buffering, compression, or edge aggregation strategy.

Configuration state management: Dynamic updates to evaluation period, sampling rate, or measurement mode may introduce race conditions or data inconsistency. Sub-level DP for FR1.3 must specify state machine and synchronization protocol.

Measurement failure detection: Current DP does not address sensor failure modes (e.g., sensor disconnection, signal loss, out-of-range readings). Sub-level DP for FR1.5 must include failure detection and safe fallback behavior.
evidence_needed: Sensor hardware specification: model, accuracy class, resolution, latency, interface type (analog, CAN, I2C), calibration procedure, and failure modes.

Measurement uncertainty analysis: quantify sensor accuracy, integration algorithm numerical stability, and cumulative error over evaluation period. Verify that reported productivity meets acceptance criterion with acceptable tolerance.
Safety alerting requirements: define threshold values for overpressure, underflow, and measurement failure; specify required alert latency and Safety Services subscription protocol.
Telemetry message schema: define cumulative throughput data format, transmission frequency (per evaluation period or periodic incremental updates), and network bandwidth estimate.

Configuration protocol specification: define Tasking interface contract for dynamic parameter updates; specify whether Vehicle can apply new parameters without service interruption or if restart is required.
Verification and test plan: specify test conditions, reference standards, acceptance tolerances, and test procedures for cumulative throughput measurement during representative operations.
Safety-critical design review: conduct FMEA or HARA to identify measurement failure modes, safety alerting dependencies, and mitigation strategies. Document safety case.
